name: Alto del Peñón Loop from Truchas
name_original: Los cauces del Eria, Truchillas y Cabrera — Circular en la Comarca de Sanabria
description: Challenge yourself on the Alto del Peñón Loop from Truchas, a difficult 37.3-mile touring cycling route with 4802 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Truchas, New Mexico, offers routes through the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, characterized by significant elevation changes and high-altitude terrain. The region features primeval spruce/fir forests, aspen glades, and views of the Truchas Peaks, which rise approximately 5,000 feet above the village. Located at about 8,000 feet above sea level, the area provides expansive views and challenging climbs for cyclists. Traditional acequias and rivers like the Rio Truchas also contribute to the natural landscape. Best touring cycling routes around Truchas The most popular touring cycling route is Encinedo and Truchas Circular Route, a 26.2 miles (42.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 23 minutes to complete. This difficult route features over 3,339 feet (1,018 meters) of ascent. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Alto del Peñón Loop from Truchas, a difficult 37.3 miles (60.1 km) path. This route includes over 4,799 feet (1,464 meters) of elevation gain, offering panoramic mountain views. Local touring cyclists also love the Puebla de Sanabria Castle – Old Town of Puebla de Sanabria loop from Puebla de Sanabria, a 36.0 miles (57.9 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 4 hours 19 minutes. Touring cycling around Truchas is defined by high-altitude roads, significant climbs, and mountain views. The network offers options for difficult ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 12 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Truchas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Truchas?
A: Komoot currently features a selection of three touring cycling routes around Truchas. These routes are designed to showcase the region's unique landscapes and offer challenging rides through the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while touring cycling around Truchas?
A: Touring cyclists in Truchas can expect dramatic scenery, including primeval spruce/fir forests, aspen glades, and expansive views of the Truchas Peaks, which rise significantly above the village. The routes often feature high-altitude terrain, traditional acequias, and views of rivers like the Rio Truchas, all set against the backdrop of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.

Q: What is the typical elevation gain on touring cycling routes in Truchas?
A: Given Truchas's high-altitude location at about 8,000 feet above sea level, touring cycling routes in the area feature significant elevation changes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1035220529/encinedo-and-truchas-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Encinedo and Truchas Circular Route&lt;/a&gt; includes over 3,339 feet (1,018 meters) of ascent, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1404139483/alto-del-penon-loop-from-truchas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Peñón Loop from Truchas&lt;/a&gt; boasts over 4,799 feet (1,464 meters) of elevation gain.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes available in Truchas?
A: Yes, there are circular touring cycling routes available. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1035220529/encinedo-and-truchas-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Encinedo and Truchas Circular Route&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 26.2 miles (42.2 km) and offers a challenging ride through the local terrain. Another notable loop is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1404139483/alto-del-penon-loop-from-truchas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Peñón Loop from Truchas&lt;/a&gt;, a longer 37.3-mile (60.1 km) path with panoramic mountain views.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks along the touring cycling routes?
A: While cycling around Truchas, you can encounter various natural features and points of interest. Highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5431418" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Truchillas River Wooden Bridge&lt;/a&gt; and the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5542912" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Waterfall and Bridge of the Arroyo del Silencio&lt;/a&gt;. The routes also offer views of the dramatic Sangre de Cristo Mountains and the Truchas Peaks.

Q: Can I expect to see wildlife while touring cycling in Truchas?
A: The area around Truchas is rich in wildlife. Cyclists may have opportunities to observe various bird species, muskrats, turtles, coyotes, deer, and even bighorn sheep, especially in the more remote sections of the routes.

Q: Is Truchas suitable for family touring cycling?
A: The touring cycling routes around Truchas are generally rated as difficult due to significant elevation changes and high-altitude terrain. There are currently no easy or moderate routes listed, which might make them less suitable for families with young children or those seeking a more relaxed ride.

Q: Are touring cycling routes in Truchas dog-friendly?
A: While the natural environment around Truchas is generally welcoming to dogs, specific regulations for bringing dogs on cycling routes can vary, especially within protected areas or private lands. It's advisable to check local guidelines and ensure your dog is accustomed to long, challenging rides and high altitudes before embarking on a tour.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Truchas?
A: The high altitude of Truchas means that spring, summer, and early fall generally offer the most favorable conditions for touring cycling. During these seasons, you can enjoy clear, expansive views and more predictable weather. Winters can bring snow and colder temperatures, making many routes challenging or inaccessible.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: Truchas is a historic village with a small, vibrant community. While specific cafes directly on every route might be limited, the village itself, and other small settlements along the High Road to Taos, may offer opportunities for refreshments and local cuisine. It's recommended to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer routes.

Q: What are the challenges of touring cycling in Truchas?
A: The primary challenges for touring cyclists in Truchas include significant elevation gains and high-altitude riding, with routes often starting around 8,000 feet above sea level. The terrain can be demanding, and riders should be prepared for sustained climbs and descents. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24550068" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puebla de Sanabria Castle – Old Town of Puebla de Sanabria loop from Puebla de Sanabria&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, covers 36 miles with over 4,300 feet of ascent.

Q: What makes Truchas a unique destination for touring cyclists?
A: Truchas offers a unique blend of challenging high-altitude cycling, breathtaking mountain views, and a rich cultural experience. Situated along the scenic High Road to Taos, cyclists can immerse themselves in the dramatic landscapes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, explore a historic village with an artistic community, and encounter diverse ecosystems, making it a distinctive and memorable destination.
tips: Truchas is the head of a municipality that brings together many towns: Baillo, Corporales, La Cuesta, Cunas, Iruela, Manzaneda, Pozos, Quintanilla de Yuso, Truchas, Truchillas, Valdavido, Villar del Monte and Villarino. In the 19th century, the lands passed into the hands of large owners, which forced the emigration of many of its inhabitants. One of them, from Iruela, emigrated to London and became a watchmaker, later manufacturing the famous clocks in Astorga or the one in Madrid's Puerta del Sol.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Alto del Peñón Loop by touring bicycle?
faqs.answer: This challenging touring cycling route usually takes around 4 hours and 54 minutes to complete. However, your actual time may vary depending on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this touring cycling route?
faqs.answer: The Alto del Peñón Loop is rated as difficult. It features a significant and sustained climb of 14.4 kilometers with a vertical gain of 725 meters, reaching a summit at 1835 meters above sea level. An average gradient of 5.1% means it requires good to very good fitness.

faqs.question: Is the Alto del Peñón Loop suitable for beginner touring cyclists?
faqs.answer: Given its significant elevation gain and sustained climb, this route is not recommended for beginners. It's best suited for experienced touring cyclists with a good level of fitness.

faqs.question: Where does the Alto del Peñón Loop from Truchas officially begin?
faqs.answer: The loop typically starts and ends in Truchas Village. Parking is generally available within the village, allowing for convenient access to the route.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for touring cycling on this loop?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features mostly well-paved surfaces, especially for the main ascent to El Peñón / Puerto de Vizcodillo. While the broader region may include unpaved sections, this specific loop is designed for touring bicycles on roads.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the Alto del Peñón Loop?
faqs.answer: You can expect picturesque views characteristic of the Montes de León region. The route is designed to explore the beautiful surrounding landscapes, offering natural beauty throughout the ride.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes by several interesting points. You'll start from Truchas Village, ascend towards Puerto El Peñón, and may also encounter Puerto Peña Aguda and Roman Canals Network depending on the exact loop chosen.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Alto del Peñón Loop?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during the warmer months, from late spring to early autumn, to avoid snow and ice at higher elevations and ensure more favorable weather conditions for cycling.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Alto del Peñón Loop?
faqs.answer: As this is primarily a road cycling route, dogs are generally not restricted. However, always ensure your dog is well-behaved and, if off-leash, under strict voice control, especially when encountering other cyclists or vehicles.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or need to pay entrance fees to cycle this route in Sierra de la Cabrera?
faqs.answer: Generally, no specific permits or entrance fees are required for touring cycling on public roads within the Sierra de la Cabrera region for this route. However, always respect local regulations and private property.

faqs.question: What gear is recommended for touring cycling this challenging route?
faqs.answer: Given the significant climb and distance, it's recommended to have a well-maintained touring bicycle with appropriate gearing for ascents. Bring plenty of water, nutrition, repair tools, and wear suitable cycling apparel for varying temperatures.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several official trails, including parts of the [CIMA LE01] Alto del Peñón, [CIMA LE13] El Morredero - Llano de las Ovejas * Nogar, and Camino del Valle.
